definition: a particle with a prepositional function before nouns, often combined with the definite particle ṅ to iṅ. We find it before nouns which in the sentence function as grammatical subject (esp. before place-names), as grammatical object either primary or secondary, as qualification of another noun (sih iṅ widhi; sinwam iṅ aśoka; tĕṅah i tal ika; kālapan iṅ wwai). It is used as preposition with the meaning ``in'', ``on'' (humaḍĕp i sor iṅ lĕmah), ``by'', ``through'', ``with''. See Zoet#mulder, De Taal van het Ādiparwa; id. Ba#hasa Parwa. Passim in all texts.

definition: demonstrative pronoun: that, those (of what is at some distance from the speaker); often: he, she, it (equiv. to a personal pronoun of the third person). For the difference between ika and iki see the remarks under iki. Ika is used to refer to what has been said and iki to words which are to follow. Ika passim in all texts. See Bahasa Parwa II p. 40. Also tika, nika.

definition: demonstrative pronoun: this, these, this here, this now (of what is near the speaker in either place or time). The difference from ika, which points to what is further away, is sometimes very slight or not demonstrable. In any case it seems dangerous to draw conclusions from the use of iki and not ika. Also

The basic meaning appears to be ``to pin between arm and body'', and thus carry or drag; carrying on the hip (cf ModJ) can be included in this. The ``keṇḍit'' found in the description of battles, however, still poses a problem: ``to drag along, sweep away''? or: ``pressed, crushed to death''?

definition: (Skt change of voice, internal motion, motion of various parts of the body as indicating the intentions; hint, sign, gesture; aim, real but covert purpose) external expression (by gestures, looks, etc), subtly indicating or suggesting internal sentiments or intentions; way of appearing or acting; finesses, subtleties, secrets. Also adj. = iṅgitajña; kawīṅgita beside kawīṅgitajña; śāstrawit iṅgita; wruh iṅgita beside wruh riṅ iṅgita. In the latter case prob. blending with wruh iṅ gita (gīta).

definition: the basic meaning seems to be ``something done at the same time as something else'' (cf ModJ). For the derivatives, with the proper reserve the following is proposed: humiras-hiras, miras-miras, ``doing several things at the same time'', > unsteady, wavering, moving to and fro (cf cala, capala); aṅiras etc ``to do st. at the same instant, all at once'', > make short work of, finish, finish off (with a meaning close to that of aṅlūd, see lūd).
